title: Should you deduct mortgage debt from your savings when calculating zakat?
tags: do I deduct mortgage from zakat
stanceSummary: No. You do not deduct long-term debt (like a mortgage) from your zakatable wealth. Debts are of two types: short-term/immediate and long-term. A mortgage is a long-term debt paid over decades. If we allowed deducting the full remaining mortgage (e.g., $250,000), most homeowners would never pay zakat for 30 years — this goes against the maqasid (goals) of the Sharia. You should pay zakat on your savings that have been held for a year, regardless of outstanding long-term debts. Yasir Qadhi also notes he has a longer lecture with Dr. Hatem al-Haj on mortgages, where they concluded that mortgages should be avoided unless there is genuine need and no Islamic alternative.
transcript:
## Summary of Yasir Qadhi's Position

In a [2021 Rapid Fire Q&A video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZKPP1H_dKk8) (starting at [15:30](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZKPP1H_dKk8&t=930)), Yasir Qadhi addressed whether mortgage debt should be deducted from savings when calculating zakat.

- **No**, you do **not** deduct long-term debt from your zakatable wealth
- Debts are of **two types**: short-term/immediate and long-term
- A mortgage is a **long-term debt** — it is understood that you pay it off over a very lengthy period of time
- If we allowed deducting the full remaining mortgage (e.g., $250,000), most homeowners would **never pay zakat for 30 years** — this goes against the **maqasid (goals) of the Sharia**
- You should pay zakat on your **savings that have been held for a year**, regardless of outstanding long-term debts
- Yasir Qadhi also has a longer lecture with **Dr. Hatem al-Haj** on mortgages, where they concluded mortgages should be **avoided** unless there is genuine need and no Islamic alternative

---

## Full Lecture Transcript (Cleaned)

### The Question: Deducting Mortgage from Zakat — [15:30](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZKPP1H_dKk8&t=930)

Brother Hashim from Virginia says that he has a home mortgage that he has to pay off, and he is asking: for the sake of zakat, should he deduct the debt from the amount of his savings to calculate zakat?

### A Note on Mortgages — [15:47](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZKPP1H_dKk8&t=947)

With regards to the issue of home mortgages, I have given a much longer lecture — a conversation that I had with Dr. Hatem al-Haj. You can look this up; it is an hour-long conversation. And we concluded that really, mortgages should be avoided unless there is some type of need or strong need or necessity, and there is no Islamic alternative.

Nonetheless, the brother is not asking about that. He is basically saying that he already has a mortgage and he is asking: does he deduct the debt of the mortgage?

### The Ruling: No Deduction for Long-Term Debt — [16:34](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZKPP1H_dKk8&t=994)

So suppose the house costs — he still has $250,000 left on it — and of course he might have savings and cash and whatnot come time of zakat. Should he deduct $250,000 from his total quantity that he has in the bank and then give zakat on that?

The response is that **no, you do not deduct** what is called a **long-term debt**. You see, the debts that are owed are of two types: immediate short-term, and long-term. Long-term is a debt that it is understood you can pay off in a very lengthy period of time.

### Why This Goes Against the Sharia's Goals — [17:00](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZKPP1H_dKk8&t=1020)

Those types of debts — and the classic example is in fact the house mortgage — if we were to consider this when we calculate zakat, then for most of us who have a house mortgage, there would **never be any zakat for 30 years**. And that goes against the **goals of the Sharia**.

We know that we have to pay $1,500 or $2,000 per month for the mortgage, whatever it might be, and we are earning a good amount more than this. Therefore, by the time the year finishes, we have a savings amount that we have had for a year that we can give zakat on.

Therefore, to answer your question simply: no, you do **not** deduct the value of the remaining mortgage in order to calculate zakat. And Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala knows best.
alternateQuestions: Can I subtract my mortgage from zakat calculation?
Do I pay zakat if I have a mortgage?
How to calculate zakat with debt?
Does mortgage debt reduce zakatable amount?
